companyCaseStudy.title: How Netflix Cut Video Bitrate ~20% (and Saved Terabits) with Per‑Title Encoding + Open Connect
companyCaseStudy.summary: Netflix optimized its entire video delivery chain by switching from a one‑size‑fits‑all encode ladder to Per‑Title Encode Optimization and coupling it with its Open Connect CDN. The result: average bitrate reductions on the order of ~20% (up to ~50% on some titles), lower CDN egress, and measurable QoE improvements at global scale.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook
Netflix streams billions of hours of video every month. A seemingly small percentage improvement in bits-per-second becomes terabits saved across the globe. Netflix reported average bitrate reductions on the order of ~20% using Per‑Title Encode Optimization — for some titles the savings were as high as ~50% — while preserving visual quality.

## The problem
For years the common approach was a fixed "encode ladder": every title (movie/episode) was encoded into the same set of renditions (e.g., 240p@400kbps, 360p@800kbps, 720p@2.5Mbps, etc.). That’s simple operationally, but wasteful:
- Simple content (talk shows, animation) doesn't need high bitrates to look good.
- Complex content (action scenes) needs higher bitrates to avoid artifacts.
- The same ladder wastes bandwidth on simple titles and hurts quality on complex ones.

At Netflix scale, inefficiencies translate to huge CDN egress costs and worse user experience (more bitrate switches, possible rebuffering).

## The challenge
Design a reproducible encoding pipeline that:
- Produces the lowest bitrate needed to achieve perceptually equivalent quality per asset;
- Plugs into the existing CDN (Open Connect) and adaptive streaming clients (HLS/DASH/ABR);
- Scales to encode Netflix's library (tens of thousands of titles and frequent updates);
- Keeps operational complexity and storage overhead manageable.

## Solution: Per‑Title Encode Optimization + Open Connect caching
Netflix moved from a single static ladder to a per‑title approach. The core idea: analyze each asset to determine an optimal set of representations (bitrate/resolution pairs) that deliver consistent perceptual quality while minimizing bits.

High-level flow:

```mermaid
graph LR
  Ingest[Content Ingest] --> Analyze[Per-Title Analyzer]
  Analyze --> Encoder[Per-Title Encoder Farm]
  Encoder --> Origin[Origin Storage (S3/Blob)]
  Origin --> OC[Open Connect Appliances / Edge Cache]
  OC --> Client[Client (Player with ABR)]
  Client --> ABR[ABR Algorithm]
  ABR --> Client
```

What happens inside the Per‑Title Analyzer/Encoder:
- Analyze: sample the title to estimate complexity across scenes (motion, detail, texture).
- Determine a custom ladder: for this title, what resolutions + target bitrates achieve the target perceptual score (VMAF or similar).
- Encode: run multiple encodes for the title, pick the pareto‑optimal renditions.
- Publish: store renditions in origin, replicate to Open Connect Appliances (OCAs) near ISPs.

Why this pairs well with Open Connect:
- Lower average bitrate per title directly reduces egress to ISP caches and backbone.
- OCAs cache popular renditions close to viewers, reducing startup latency and rebuffering.
- Per‑Title reduces churn of ABR switching (fewer abrupt quality drops), improving QoE.

## Architectural decisions & tradeoffs
- Quality metric: Netflix relied on objective perceptual metrics (VMAF) tuned with human studies to define targets.
- Encode compute: per‑title optimization requires more encoder runs (more CPU/GPU). Netflix amortized cost by batching and using massive encoding farms — a pre-processing cost traded for ongoing CDN savings.
- Storage vs bandwidth: generating a tailored ladder sometimes increases the number of renditions for a title (more granular points), but the dominant win is lower bitrate per rendition, so net storage and egress decreased.
- Client compatibility: kept standard HLS/DASH manifests so existing players and ABR algorithms worked unchanged.

## Results (numbers and outcomes)
| Metric | Before (static ladder) | After (Per‑Title) |
|--------|------------------------:|------------------:|
| Average bitrate (catalog-wide) | baseline | ~20% lower on average |
| Bitrate reduction (simple titles) | baseline | up to ~50% for some titles |
| Visual quality (perceptual) | baseline | maintained (target VMAF) |
| CDN egress | baseline | proportional reduction (~20% less egress) |

Concrete business impact:
- Tens of percent reductions in bandwidth translate to millions of dollars saved annually at Netflix scale (every Mbps saved × millions of concurrent viewers × hours = huge savings).
- End‑user QoE improved: fewer quality switches and fewer rebuffering incidents in controlled studies.

## Before / After (story form)
Before: one ladder for all titles → overprovisioned bits on talk shows, underprovisioned on action scenes, wasted CDN capacity.
After: per‑title ladders + Open Connect edge caches → bits match content complexity, less egress, smoother playback.

## Why it matters to your systems
- At scale, micro optimizations at media layer become macro wins on infrastructure cost and UX.
- The approach is an example of shifting cost: spend more CPU/encode time up front so you save recurring network and QoE costs downstream.
- It also shows the synergy between offline preprocessing (encoding decisions) and online systems (CDN, ABR clients).

## Diagram: simplified delivery pipeline
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Content Creator Upload] --> B[Ingest & QC]
  B --> C[Per-Title Analyzer]
  C --> D[Encoder Farm (many encodes)]
  D --> E[Origin Storage]
  E --> F[Open Connect / Edge Caches]
  F --> G[Client Player (HLS/DASH + ABR)]
```

## Practical notes for implementers
- Start by instrumenting perceptual quality (VMAF) and correlate with human judgments for your content class.
- Prototype per‑title rules: detect low complexity titles and try simple, aggressive reductions first.
- Measure end‑to‑end: bandwidth saved, startup time, bitrate switches, rebuffering rate.
- Watch out for live content: per‑title precomputation works for VOD; live streaming needs different techniques (dynamic encoder ladders, real‑time ABR tuning).

## Where Netflix pushed further
- Per‑Title combined with Per‑Title+Per‑Chunk (finer granularity) and dynamic optimizer techniques to squeeze more savings from scene‑by‑scene complexity.
- Continued work on client ABR algorithms to take advantage of more efficient ladders and reduce oscillations.
keyTakeaways: Per‑Title encoding reduced average catalog bitrate by ~20% (and up to ~50% on simple titles), directly cutting CDN egress and cost.
Spend more CPU/time upfront (more encodes) to reduce recurring network costs and improve QoE — a classic shift-of-costs tradeoff.
Pair offline optimization (encoding) with CDN placement (Open Connect) and robust ABR clients to get end‑to‑end gains.

concept.title: Cache Patterns Unlocked: When to Use Cache-Aside, Write-Through, and Write-Back
concept.summary: Caching can turn a sluggish, overloaded database into a fast, resilient system — but the choice of caching pattern determines correctness, freshness, and complexity. Learn the tradeoffs between cache-aside (lazy), write-through, and write-back (write-behind) with practical examples, diagrams, and pitfalls to avoid.
concept.explanation:
## Why it matters
Many web apps are read-heavy: product pages, user profiles, leaderboards. A single hot endpoint can crush your database, causing high latency or outages. Caching reduces latency and load, but done incorrectly it creates stale data, lost writes, or thundering herds. Choosing the right cache pattern is about balancing *performance*, *consistency*, and *operational complexity*.

### Analogy: the library and the copies
- Cache-aside is like a reader who checks the nearby bookshelf (cache) and only goes to the central library (DB) if the book is missing; they then bring a copy back to the local shelf.
- Write-through is like a librarian who, when a user updates a book, writes the change to the local shelf and immediately files the update in the central archive.
- Write-back is like a local reading room where users update a copy and the room periodically syncs changes back to the central archive — fast for users, riskier for archive durability.

### How each pattern works (flow)
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Client[Client Request] --> App[App Server]
  App --> Cache{Cache}
  Cache -->|Hit| Return[Return Cached Data]
  Cache -->|Miss| DB[Database]
  DB --> App
  App --> CacheSet[Set Cache with TTL]
  CacheSet --> Return
```

This flow represents **cache-aside** (lazy loading): the app checks cache, on miss it fetches DB and populates cache.

### Sequence for writes (write-through example)
```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>App: Update Request
  App->>Cache: Update cache
  App->>DB: Write DB
  DB-->>App: Ack
  App-->>Client: Ack
```

### Comparison table
| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| Cache-aside (lazy) | Simple, minimal cache writes, easy to reason about | Stale reads between DB update and explicit cache invalidation, potential cache stampede | Read-heavy workloads, eventual consistency OK |
| Write-through | Stronger read-after-write consistency, cache always updated on writes | Higher write latency (two writes per update), more IO on write-heavy systems | Moderate write rate, need fresher reads |
| Write-back (write-behind) | Very low write latency for client, reduces DB write bursts | Risk of lost writes if cache node crashes, complex flush/durability logic | Extreme write-heavy workloads with careful durability guarantees |

### BEFORE / AFTER (example metric comparison)
| Metric | Before (DB-only) | After (Cache-aside) |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| P99 latency | 200 ms | 30 ms (cache hit) |
| Avg DB QPS | 10,000 | 2,000 (cache absorbs reads) |
| Data freshness | N/A | Eventual (depends on TTL/invalidation) |

### Practical code: cache-aside in Node.js (Redis + DB)
```js
// pseudo-code using single quotes (avoid double quotes in JSON)
async function getUser(userId) {
  const cacheKey = `user:${userId}`
  const cached = await redis.get(cacheKey)
  if (cached) return JSON.parse(cached)

  // on miss, fetch from DB
  const user = await db.query('select * from users where id = $1', [userId])
  if (user) {
    // set with TTL to limit staleness and control eviction
    await redis.set(cacheKey, JSON.stringify(user), 'EX', 60) // 60s TTL
  }
  return user
}

// update: invalidate cache (common approach)
async function updateUser(userId, patch) {
  await db.query('update users set ... where id = $1', [...])
  await redis.del(`user:${userId}`) // keep cache-aside semantics
}
```

### When to use which pattern
- Use **cache-aside** when reads dominate, you can tolerate short staleness, and you prefer simple, explicit invalidation or TTLs. It's the most common pattern for web caches.
- Use **write-through** when you need fresher reads immediately after writes and can accept the extra write latency and load. Good for moderate write rates where consistency matters.
- Use **write-back** only when you have extreme write throughput and can implement robust durability and flushing (e.g., distributed queues, ack mechanisms). It's complex and risky if durability is critical.

### Common pitfalls and mitigations
- Cache stampede (many requests miss and hit DB simultaneously): mitigate with request coalescing (mutex per key), probabilistic early recompute, or locking.
- Stale reads after DB update: ensure you either invalidate the cache on writes, use write-through, or use very short TTLs. Beware ordering: delete-then-write vs write-then-delete matters in concurrent systems.
- Lost writes in write-back: use durable queues or write-ahead logs to persist changes before acknowledging clients.
- Large objects and memory pressure: avoid caching huge blobs; store references or smaller serialized views; use appropriate eviction policies (LRU, LFU).
- Cache thrashing and hot keys: shard, add local caches, or use dedicated hot-key strategies (rate-limit, prioritize cached entries).

### Quick rules of thumb
- If reads >> writes and eventual consistency is OK: cache-aside.
- If you need stronger read-after-write guarantees: write-through.
- If you need the absolute lowest write latency and can handle complexity: write-back.

### Final checklist before choosing
- How fresh must reads be? (strong vs eventual consistency)
- What's the read/write ratio?
- Can you tolerate complexity around durability and flushes?
- Do you have hotspots or a uniform workload?

By picking the right caching pattern and addressing stampedes, invalidation order, and TTLs, you can reduce latency by orders of magnitude while keeping your data correct enough for your application's needs.
keyTakeaways: Cache-aside is simple and fits read-heavy systems where eventual consistency is acceptable.
Write-through gives fresher reads at the cost of write latency and more IO.
Write-back can reduce write latency but needs careful durability and flushing to avoid lost writes.
